item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only gripe with this one is the excessive usage of blackboxes and [REDACTED], since I don't feel they add anything to this article. Nothing too off about this one, just a seemingly harmless space amoeba and its colony just moving towards the solar system, not bothering anyone. I'd urge maybe adding a little more to this SCP as well.

Edit: When I mean add a little more, I mean more of a hook to interest the reader. Maybe elaborate further on SCP-2027-1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2027 feeds on strong concentrations of gamma radiation

Although I think that idea of space organism traveling for the space has been worked several times, for me the main problem is the gamma-radiation based life, this idea was developed more than 60 years ago in the fiction tale Talking Stone from Isaac Asimov: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Talking_Stone.

However in the tale the development of the idea is more consistent, siliconies are organism than live closer to ore of radiactive materials, as uranium, while the specimens of SCP-2027 are just traveling around the space looking for a energy source. I assume for this size that they need massive sources of gamma radiation, as from neutron stars or pulsar, then, Why are traveling closer to the Earth? and Why they are not stay closer to a pulsar or a black hole, consistent source of "food"? Or, as is conveniently expressed in the last statement, if this organism belong to the center of the galaxy, why are migrating to a region poor in resources?

I reconigze that it's a article with more of 4 years, and the author wanted to write a science-like piece , however, for the previously describe the work can't convice me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
3 weeks later, jets of the same gas emitted from SCP-2027 was emitted from SCP-2027-1 and the appendage was disconnected. SCP-2027-1 then accelerated away from SCP-2027 in the direction of the galactic center.

Instant upvote. I was already going to upvote after reading the description but this sealed the deal. Absolutely cool, simple SCP, and not overbearing like many other space-based SCPs can be (Which is not necessarily a bad thing, by the way).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Old Timer refers to someone who has been involved in a certain position for a long period of time who believes they are an "Elite" of sorts, thinking they have knowledge of everything, and that they are somehow superior than newer users.

For example, I was an Old Timer of various Garrys Mod servers, Forum Groups, and writing groups. The Writing Group is what made me an "old timer" class individual, constantly believing I was above newer members. The Forum Group only solidified this belief, making me have the mindset that I was a dictator of sorts. I was promoted to a Moderator of the group, which boosted my ego, making me feel even more like the Leader, and considering I was the last "Oldie" member left, I felt like I was the supreme master of the group.

The group died off shortly after. Everyone moved on to more productive things. I did too, and I really regret falling into that kind of mindset.

TL:DR, an Old Timer means someone who thinks just because they have stayed in a position longer than you means that they are always going to be superior to you in every single way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's a relevent post I made later on, and this one too that discusses in detail some issues with the SCP that was made after it was posted (and has since changed.)

Long story short, if the Foundation has telescopes to see this thing that's outside of our solar system, containment of any SCP should be a non-issue because the tech level of the Foundation is simply that advanced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What even is an old timer anymore?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You should care about it for the same reason you care about a statue that moves when you're not looking.
You aren't supposed to.
I guess I should remind everyone that this isn't NASA. This isn't Space X.
This is The SCP Foundation. The most powerful organization on the planet. If you can contain nearly 3,000 SCP's, keep the world from being obliterated, and manage all of those said sites without the public having much knowledge about it, then you can also probably build some great telescopes.
Hell, they have a machine that can completely replenish the population (SCP-2000), so I wouldn't doubt any of the Foundations capability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering that I've been here some five years, and I see unique and original shit posted all the time, this is utter bull.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, you can never make something "original", or something that will make old timers happy.
They will always say:

  • "Its been done before."
  • "Stale idea."
  • "Cliche."
  • "Too many of these SCP's."
  • "Good writing, as for originality, not good."

They'll also probably say something along the lines of "Its not clinical enough."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice to see a Giant Space Thing What /Doesn't/ Want to Kill us. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was reading a book about a space elevator trip, and things like squid that lived in space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've defeated me at making this scary. I never thought of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I the only person who has spotted the fridge logic implied threat at the end of the article?

1. The creatures feed on high concentrations of gamma radiation
2. Gamma radiation destroys earth-lifeforms. Some have even (semi-seriously) postulated it as a cause for some previous mass-extinction events.
3. The creatures are migrating towards us

Sounds like they might know of something which is about to happen that we don't…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just an odd question, how did you come up with this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Predator/Prey relationships happen when one organism errr.. 'discovers' it's easier to get nutrients from tearing apart someone else rather than making their own from the environment. It seems to me.. any animal that can make matter directly from energy for propulsion and for it's metabolisim has already figured out the perfect way of feeding so predators probably never evolved. Also given how large space actually is predators finding these things is so remote that any that evolved probably starved.

The idea is awesome on it's own though. Where did these things come from? Maybe a few centimeters of rock on a planet blew up with bacteria that could absorb radiation like plants do with sunlight and they snowballed from there over zillions of years?

I like the idea that the threat comes from the fact in a few hundred years we may be witness to an interstellar wildebeest migration/stampede. It would be a shame to learn at that point that they can convert matter into energy to absorb.
Plus 1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the point of the article. If it isn't interesting enough to you, that's fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can't fix the fact that it's literally just a space cow feeding off gamma radiation, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you may be missing something. I fixed all of the logic failures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoting for the reasons specified above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hooray! We're at +10!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Niven. Sci-fi classic.

Google it, you might like it if you like "high-concept" scifi and don't mind somewhat crude characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a weird zooiod in space. Good enough for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Safe means you can put in it a box and it won't do anything. That is not true of this.
Euclid it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Found it hard to understand until I took another close read at this. It is a very interesting idea and the concept sound. Also, since it doesn't appear to be hostile at all, I doubt it being classified as euclid is needed but being how unpredictable things are in this world it may be justified. Just be sure you check your facts, make accurate statements and correct a few errors.

Otherwise, +1. Just be sure to do the best you can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just barely upvotable for me, and I expect it'll be one of those ones that hovers around the negative/positive range forever, but it does just enough to make me like it that I couldn't in good conscious not give it an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That fridge logic doesn't work for me, give it seems to be based on assuming that the space NEEDS to have the same concept of ecosistem than we do.
I mean, -why- would something need to fill any of those niches? Is a whole different system, they may not have the prey/predator concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meant it to be a siphonophore space cow. Not a siphonophore space jellyfish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Technically speaking, being a Space Jellyfish is what makes it anomalous. Energy to Matter conversion is just icing on the cake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It mostly works on fridge logic. If this fills the niche of a cow in the vacuum ecosystem, what fills the niche of a lion, or a crocodile?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The gamma rays to gases thing is what makes it anomalous. The Foundation has no idea.
Also, all problems fixed. The "closest approach in 1986" thing was something from the draft that I forgot to edit out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At my computer now…

Another line-by-line!
Time to be nitpicky!

200-350 KM

SI measurements for kilometers are in lower case.

SCP-2027's primary propulsion method is undetermined.

This is almost flat-out wrong, I can guarantee it. Newton's laws of momentum can be summarized as this: things that move want to keep moving, and things not moving want to stay put, unless something else keeps them from doing so. Things on Earth stop moving because of something called friction- the air, water, ground, all sap momentum from objects, eventually causing it to stop.

Space is basically… empty. Sure, there's subatomic particles everywhere, but the average density of the universe is less than one atom per meter. So, anything moving is gonna keep moving for a VERY long time due to the near-total lack of friction. Simply put, it doesn't need any propulsion systems to keep moving once it has started to move. Gas jets can help provide some momentum, and aid with course corrections. Major momentum will likely occur with gravity slingshots- basically pass by close enough to a large planet to be effected by it's gravity, and then change your course at the right time to be flung out. Similar property applies if you tie something to a rope, spin it really fast, and then let go of the rope.

SCP-2027 was detected by the [REDACTED] Gamma Ray observatory on ██/█/19██. The complete lack of gamma radiation near SCP-2027 was logged into public record.

The bolded part is really iffy. The thing about Gamma Rays is that they are basically EVERYWHERE. Case in point, a photo of the moon using a gamma ray detector. The big white thing is the moon, everything else is what amounts to background static from other sources of gamma radiation.

In order to spot this sucker, the gamma ray observitory would have to, by chance, be observing a stellar formation using its gamma ray detectors when the SCP came through. Depending on what it was looking at, the field of view would be extremely small, and requires exposure times lasting days or weeks. The thing would basically have to blunder into the field of view, stay in the field of view for a long enough time to get noticed by the astronomers, and NOT get passed off as some kind of software bug.

Part of the reason that this is a bit iffy is due to an optical illusion dealing with distance. Objects in the foreground appear to move faster than objects in the background. Depending on what the actual target of the observatory was, it might appear as a gamma-ray-less streak accross the field of view (and likely a software glitch) or it wouldn't appear at all due to moving too fast compared to the background, aside from the picture not being as clear as it should be. And this is assuming that it doesn't pass over areas that the scientists would be filtering out, so they wouldn't notice the missing gamma rays.

You addressed some of this with

SCP-2027 appears to be traveling directly parallel to the Solar System's orbit around the galactic center

but it only works if the guys decided to look along the galactic rim, and not a distant pulsar.

Hell, the scientists should be recruited for spotting the sucker, as in the 80's, it was about 1.89210568 × 1011 kilometers from the Earth. By comparsion, Pluto is about 5.90638 × 109 kilometers from Earth. This is very close by comparison. Depending on the technology of when it was spotted and how large the field of view was, it would be less than a pixel of no gamma rays.

SCP-2027's closest approach to the Solar System was in 198█, when it entered within 0.02 light year of Earth. Current projections show no risk of SCP-2027 impacting Earth or any other body in the Solar System. SCP-2027's closest approach to the Solar System will be 0.009 light years if no course corrections are made.

This doesn't make sense. If it's going to be CLOSER, then the figure at 198█ was the distance it was measured at to get the projection- it's heading towards us, so why would it be closer then further away, before coming back closer?

On █/██/20██, Probe ██A-2027 has detected multiple sporadic emissions of Argon, Methane, and Hydrogen from the areas SCP-2027 has passed through. These signatures are of compositions identical to SCP-2027's maneuvering jets.

Was this what you meant by more? If the Foundation could spot the first one, they could certainly spot the others using the same technique as soon as the probe noted the emissions.

Also, if it eats gamma rays, how is it picking up the raw materials for the gases it emits?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I concur with the neutral voters. It's a decent, kinda sorta interesting idea, but it lacks a real "wow" factor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got it. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure thing. It'll have to be later, though. I'm on my phone right now, and doing anything significant like that is a big pain.

Don't worry, it should survive another day or two, unless all the no-votes turn into downvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I got rid of all the logical impossibilities in here. Can you spot more so I can weed them out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crap! Fix!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It wasn't the coolest but it was still a neat idea. I don't get all the hate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On █/█/201█, a Foundation-operated probe launched on █/█/19██ rendezvoused with SCP-2027.

Basically, sometime on or before September 9th, 2019, a probe arrived at the SCP's location, after being launched sometime during the 20th century. Again, Voyager I was launched in 1977, and entered the Heliopause in either 2012 or 2013. It's still closer to us than it would be to the SCP, and this is with the afore-mentioned gravity boost.

In order to arrive in the timespan you're giving it, the probe would need to have been launched long before any telescope that could have spotted it was made. Hell, it would likely have to have been launched long before humanity invested into spaceflight. That is a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agh! Attack of the Downvotes!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read the last Addendum. I took careful pains to censor the date the probe left Earth. Remember. The article does not necessarily have to be set now. Also, I changed the detection method in the final draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entire point is that it is a long way away and completely harmless, but more are coming, and THAT is the threat.

A doctor's hunch isn't enough to get that point across, especially when all that is documented is the one object.

It also doesn't explain how it was discovered in the first place. Getting in the way of a telescope, as you described it in the draft page, stretches my suspension of disbelief. The Hubble has a hard enough time getting a picture of Pluto, which is far closet and larger than this object.

The Voyager probes were launched in 1977, and Voyager I reached the heliopause a year or two ago. And both Voyager I and II had a gravity boost caused by a rare alignment of the outer planets to speed them up. There has not been enough time to get a probe to the object, nor was the technology developed enough to see it if it was launched any time during the twentieth century, even assuming the Foundation's tech level is a decade or two ahead of everyone else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entire point is that it is a long way away and completely harmless, but more are coming, and THAT is the threat.
Here's a link to the Draft thread. http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-840359/space-borne-organism-first-keter-draft
And I indeed reviewed it in chat, where people said it was mostly good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still a downvote. .02 LY is still a huge distance away, and, if I recall correctly, beyond the Heliopsuse, the very edge of the solar system. It's still way too small to see. Even if it wasn't, it's too small to qualify as a dwarf planet.

Author, it has become painfully clear that this SCP was not ready to be mainlisted. You should have set up a sandbox and submitted a link to the drafts and critiques forum and chat for review and critiquing.

Furthermore, you need a better understanding of space terminology before you can accurately use it. There are several glaring errors, like the ones I mentioned, that harm the quality of your SCP. Do the research yourself, or ask for help on the forums.

That said, you are on a good track for this SCP, as it has not been down voted to oblivion just yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hook added.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Polishing is done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What are you referencing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looks like one of Niven's starseeds to me.

Now keep an eye out for an Outsider ship following it - maybe we can get a good deal on a hyperdrive shunt…

No vote for now, since you're still polishing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited distance to make it more plausible (still outside the orbit of Sedna)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As has been said in various forms above, the science behind this one is extremely questionable, starting with how the hell the Foundation even observed this, and just getting worse (the Foundation sent a probe 2 trillion km away? Really?) as this goes along. So much so that the whole thing just comes across as rather ridiculous.

"Giant alien organism" could work on its own, I suppose, but it needs a lot more detail and weirdness than what you've given it here to be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Grabs a polishing brush*
Editing now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I certainly agree that one light year is too far away to be a threat, especially at the speed it's moving. You might want to think about reducing the distance or increasing the speed; nothing crazy (don't push it to 1/10th light speed, or move it to the edge of the solar system), but change it in a way to make it important.

I really like the final note as well, you should have run with that idea. We currently only see one of these things, but much further away we are starting to see many, many more. They are migrating, and some might end up only a few billion or trillion kilometers from here. That's your threat: in two or three hundred years we may have tens of thousands of these things cruising by our solar system. The idea of them being non hostile is a good one, I like that they just float around in the universe. The threat would just be from exposure, or the slim chance of a few actually coming too close.

I remember reading this on the draft forum and liking the idea, but I still think it needed some more polish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
much better! +1 from me.

I think some of the others may be after a bit more of a hook, possible some side effect witnessed to planets / starts / other celestial objects that are close to 2027

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. It's pretty easy to envision a spaceborne organism. I doubt it would be as active as this, or likely to leave a solar system, but I don't see anything inherently impossible about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made an addition with a Foundation space probe, the true villain of this story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Iffy science. Yet interesting idea! No vote for now.

XX/X/200█

"██/█/200█".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… it is a giant jellyfish made up of smaller jellyfish?

As far as things go, this article is kind of dull. Nothing actually happens with this organism. It's just floating off in space.

It's important to note that 1 light year is almost 10 trillion kilometers, and almost a quarter of the distance to our next closest star Alpha Centauri. With this distance, calling it Keter or even Euclid seems unnecessary. There needs to be something else to justify its classification or it needs to be lowered.

Gamma Ray Burst should not be capitalized. Don't capitalize the second article A in Addendum 2.

Say that the mass of matter was designated SCP-2027-1 instead of putting it in parentheses(which, should also not be capitalized).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had to downvote when i read:

it is impossible to contain

physical containment may not be possible, but keeping it out of public record and use of disinformation is a form of containment.

SCP-2027 was upgraded to Keter at this time.

but its still Euclid. it would probably be better if you mentioned a reasonable distance that would result in a reclassification, or alternatively if more instances of 2027 where spotted.

Other than that I like the idea a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What, that's not enough? "An alien thing off in space doing something" is by the very merit of its existence a major challenge to everything that non-anomalous science knows to be true.
